name: Platform-neutral procurement language for AltID integration
consequences: Direct effect: Embedding platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurement compels vendors to decouple trust anchors from OS signals and pursue compliant fallback options. Downstream: procurement timelines may lengthen and incumbent platform providers may push back, requiring strong governance to manage transition costs and continuity.
options: Embed platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurement specifications, with explicit conformance testing and fallback criteria.
Create a parallel procurement track for open standards identity components and pilot interoperability tests.
Commission an EU-aligned external review of AltID procurement risks focusing on vendor lock-in and continuity.
review: Platform-neutral procurement vs incumbent platform incentives. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs and supplier lobbying.

name: Standards and interoperability pressure
consequences: Direct effect: Elevates platform neutral interoperability as a policy objective, enhancing legitimacy for fallback paths. Downstream: progress depends on EU and national standards cycles, potentially delaying concrete procurement changes.
options: Lead a Danish standards working group to publish platform-neutral interoperability profiles for digital identity across NSIS and eIDAS contexts.
Advocate formal EU commentary to include certified fallback paths and portability in the European Digital Identity Wallet framework.
Draft regulatory guidance for procurement that requires supplier diversification and continuity planning in identity services.
review: Standards and interoperability pressure vs slow standards adoption. Weakness: The options assume rapid consensus that may not materialize.

name: Resilience framing and public narrative
consequences: Direct effect: Shifts public policy debate toward resilience, continuity, and supplier diversification, strengthening political support for platform-neutral options. Downstream: risk that messaging becomes adversarial or triggers cost concerns among stakeholders.
options: Publish a resilience case study comparing platform dependence with platform-neutral fallback scenarios for Danish public services.
Commission a risk assessment focused on continuity of service under platform policy changes and vendor transitions.
Organize stakeholder messaging workshops with civil society to refine resilience narratives and counter arguments about convenience.
review: Resilience framing vs convenience-oriented narratives. Weakness: The options may overemphasize risk without concrete short-term benefits.

name: Technical demonstrators and evidence scaffolding
consequences: Direct effect: Provides credible, standards-based demonstrations of feasibility and potential fallback paths. Downstream: demonstrations alone may not drive procurement without formal certification.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators: one mobile-oriented using WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, another browser-based fallback path.
Document threat modeling, interoperability tests, and security properties to support procurement arguments.
Publish design notes and open standards artifacts to enable external validation and reuse.
review: Evidence-driven demonstrations vs production-readiness expectations. Weakness: The demonstrations may not translate into procurement criteria without certification.

name: Coalition governance and stakeholder engagement
consequences: Direct effect: Builds legitimacy and alignment across civil society, privacy advocates, and policymakers. Downstream: governance overhead and potential delays if consensus is hard to reach.
options: Formalize a Danish coalition with civil society, privacy advocates, and Folketinget members including a governance charter.
Create an institutional engagement plan with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator, with quarterly review milestones.
Organize a threat-model exercise with stakeholders to surface blockers and decision gates.
review: Coalition legitimacy vs governance overhead. Weakness: The coalition may struggle to maintain commitment and decision speed.

name: Regulatory baseline and procurement readiness
consequences: Direct effect: Establishes a formal policy foundation and potential pathways for regulatory alignment. Downstream: progress may be slow and depend on political will and legislative timetables.
options: Draft a policy note outlining regulatory requirements for platform-neutral fallback authentication aligned with eIDAS and NSIS.
Propose a regulatory sandbox to pilot platform-neutral fallback access under government oversight.
Submit a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen detailing continuity and supplier-concentration risk.
review: Regulatory baseline vs political feasibility. Weakness: Policy sandbox progress is uncertain and may delay concrete outcomes.

name: Funding governance and risk management
consequences: Direct effect: Improves budget discipline and risk sharing across phases, supporting credible execution. Downstream: adds administrative overhead and potential delays if milestones are contested.
options: Institute milestone-based disbursement with phase gates tied to published feasibility and demonstrator readiness.
Create contingency financing plan and pursue external grants to buffer funding volatility.
Explicitly allocate legal and procurement support in phase budgets to reduce friction during transitions.
review: Funding discipline vs administrative overhead. Weakness: Milestone-based disbursement may be brittle if milestones are contested.

name: Platform-neutral fallback mandate in AltID procurement specs
consequences: Requiring a platform-neutral fallback path directly reduces dependency on Apple/Google for critical services and creates a clearer governance path for continuity. Downstream, this increases procurement complexity, tightens certification requirements, and may trigger resistance from existing platform partners who fear market share erosion.
options: Codify a platform-neutral fallback requirement in all AltID-related procurement contracts, with explicit success criteria and exit clauses for non-compliance.
Pilot a cross-agency platform-neutral authentication layer using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect in three public services to validate interoperability.
Institute independent verification of fallback trust anchors through a certified third-party security assessment on an annual cycle.
review: Platform-neutral procurement standards vs vendor-centric ecosystems. Weakness: The options may underestimate the procurement-cycle delays and the risk of non-compliance penalties.

name: Open standards validation program across Danish agencies
consequences: Establishes shared testing resources and governance to validate interoperability, enabling quicker rollouts of fallback paths across agencies. Downstream, it requires coordination across departments, potential scope creep, and sustained funding to maintain test harnesses.
options: Formalize a cross-agency open standards validation program for identity interoperability and shared test harness.
Establish a standing open-source reference implementation to accelerate adoption and reduce vendor lock-in.
Create a regulatory rubric to evaluate reliance on platform-integrated signals during procurement.
review: Open standards validation vs. agency autonomy. Weakness: The options may neglect competing agency priorities and budget cycles.

name: Independent security validation and red-team testing for fallback paths
consequences: Brings external credibility to fallback approaches and reveals gaps before production. Downstream, it increases project cost and schedule pressure, and may expose sensitive architectural weaknesses.
options: Convene a quarterly independent security review with public-sector red-team exercises focused on fallback authentication.
Establish a certification program for fallback paths with limited live-service exposure and defined safety margins.
Commission a cross-border cryptographic agility assessment to ensure resilience against platform policy changes.
review: External validation vs. internal biases. Weakness: The options may rely on external parties whose findings could be slow to integrate.

name: EU-aligned interoperability and regulatory engagement strategy
consequences: Aligns Danish obligations with EU Digital Identity Wallet expectations, increasing legitimacy and access to EU funding. Downstream, it may require significant lobbying and compromise with incumbents; risk of regulatory capture.
options: Lobby for EU-aligned requirements in European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks to support platform neutrality.
Submit formal EU stakeholder consultation responses advocating fallback pathways and governance.
Establish a joint Danish-EU standards task force to harmonize NSIS deployment for AltID.
review: EU alignment vs national sovereignty. Weakness: EU timelines may be slow and risk misalignment with Danish procurement cycles.

name: Public-interest risk-sharing and resilience funding mechanisms
consequences: Creates a financial buffer to support experiments and procurement language shifts, reducing the risk of single-vendor bottlenecks. Downstream, it can complicate governance and require new oversight structures; potential for misuse if not tightly scoped.
options: Create a risk-sharing fund with government and civil-society partners to underwrite platform-neutral procurement experiments.
Introduce milestone-based funding with stop-work rights if critical dependencies become unmanageable.
Leverage EU innovation funding programs to support non-production demonstrators and procurement language.
review: Funding-based incentives vs. accountability. Weakness: Without clear metrics, funds may be misallocated or captured by existing operators.

name: Domestic capability development and supplier diversification plan
consequences: Develops internal Danish capability to design, test, and validate alternative trust anchors, reducing dependence on foreign platform providers. Downstream, it requires long-term investment in talent and regulatory alignment; initial results may be modest and slow.
options: Launch a domestic capability-building program to develop secure, platform-agnostic verification components within Danish research institutions.
Establish a Danish supplier registry for platform-neutral authentication components to reduce dependency on Apple/Google.
Incentivize cross-border partnerships with Nordic vendors to spread risk and share best practices.
review: Diversification vs. national control. Weakness: The plan may face regulatory hurdles and limited immediate impact.

name: Fallback authentication pathway governance and procurement language
consequences: Direct effect: Establishes a legally binding fallback authentication path that remains usable if platform signals change, reducing single-vendor risk. Downstream: procurement cycles may lengthen and certification overhead increases, but continuity of critical public services improves; potential pushback from incumbent vendors may complicate negotiations.
options: Codify a platform-neutral fallback path as a formal requirement in AltID procurement language with explicit credentialing criteria.
Create a regulatory sandbox to test fallback authentication under simulated platform outages across multiple identity providers.
Incorporate ongoing interoperability audits and external security reviews into contract milestones for fallback components.
review: Platform-centric trust signals vs. fallback continuity. Weakness: The options may underestimate transition costs.

name: Open standards-based demonstrator program for platform independence
consequences: Direct effect: Demonstrators provide tangible evidence of platform independence using standards like WebAuthn/OpenID Connect on diverse devices. Downstream: strengthens policy debate on platform neutrality and informs procurement, but may raise expectations beyond feasibility and complicate risk budgeting.
options: Launch a non-production demonstrator program using GrapheneOS and standard WebAuthn/OpenID Connect to authenticate against a simulated public identity environment.
Develop a browser-based fallback variant to demonstrate cross-platform compatibility and resilience.
Publish technical findings and evaluation criteria to accelerate policy discussions with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator.
review: Platform independence vs. production-readiness. Weakness: Demonstrators may not capture real-world integration costs or regulatory constraints.

name: EU regulatory alignment for platform-neutral AltID requirements
consequences: Direct effect: Aligns national strategy with EU expectations for interoperability and vendor diversification. Downstream: may increase negotiation complexity and extend timelines as consensus is sought across member states.
options: Lobby for inclusion of platform-neutrality criteria in AltID procurement at EU working groups.
Draft EU-consistent requirements that reference NSIS standards and eIDAS2 portable identity principles.
Coordinate with member states to share best practices and harmonize fallback transparency obligations.
review: EU alignment vs. national autonomy. Weakness: Achieving consensus across diverse stakeholders is uncertain and can slow decision-making.

name: Continuity-of-service resilience testing and contingency playbooks
consequences: Direct effect: Formalizes testing of resilience against platform outages and policy shifts. Downstream: requires budget for drills and incident response resources, and may divert funds from other improvements.
options: Institutionalize periodic resilience drills that simulate outages of app-store availability and platform integrity signals.
Develop a cross-institution contingency playbook detailing switch-over steps, data-access controls, and user notification procedures.
Commission independent reviews of continuity claims and publish results to public accountability channels.
review: Continuity vs. cost. Weakness: Drills must be carefully scoped to avoid overrun and false positives; may not reflect live risk.

name: Coalition-backed procurement guardrails to prevent vendor lock-in
consequences: Direct effect: Builds political and organizational pressure to enforce open interfaces and long-term interoperability. Downstream: may face pushback from incumbents and legal risk around antitrust concerns; could slow procurement if guardrails are too strict.
options: Institutionalize open-standards requirements and sunset clauses in AltID-related procurement contracts.
Create a multi-stakeholder governance council with decision rights over vendor-switching and data portability.
Require periodic re-benchmarking of platform trust signals and third-party attestation to maintain open interoperability.
review: Lock-in risk vs. governance. Weakness: Guardrails may not be enforceable or portable across budgets and ministries, undermining impact.

name: Platform-agnostic trust anchor framework and telemetry governance
consequences: Direct effect: Explores decoupling from platform-specific trust anchors by using open standards and cross-provider telemetry. Downstream: requires a robust governance model for telemetry data, privacy considerations, and cross-provider trust assessments; may complicate certification.
options: Define a modular trust-anchor architecture using open standards with interfaces for multiple providers.
Set up a shared telemetry and attestation framework with auditable records, privacy-preserving collection, and minimal exposure.
Pilot a hardware-based or browser-based fallback anchor that can operate independently of OS-managed signals.
review: Decoupling trust anchors vs. regulator acceptance. Weakness: The approach may face regulatory uncertainty about new attestations and cross-provider data sharing.
